sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on Australian state agriculture departments (DPIRD Western Australia and Business Queensland), the Queensland low-chill stonefruit program, university horticulture extension, and industry and horticultural research sources.

core.intro: Nectarines are smooth-skinned peaches, the same species (Prunus persica) grown in exactly the same way, so everything that makes a good peach makes a good nectarine: the right variety for your winter chill, an open sunny tree, and a watch on leaf curl and brown rot. The one real difference is the fuzz-free skin, which looks and eats beautifully but gives the fruit a little less protection, so airflow, fruit thinning and clean picking matter even more. The state sections below cover the climate, growing regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes here apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Nectarines come in white or yellow flesh, and as clingstone (the flesh grips the stone) or freestone (it lifts away cleanly), exactly as peaches do. Yellow-fleshed types such as Fantasia and Flavortop are the familiar tangy-sweet nectarines, while Goldmine, White Satin and Sun Snow are prized for sweet white flesh. You can also get genetic-dwarf trees bred to stay small in a pot (Nectazee, dwarf Fantasia, Crimson Baby) and interspecific crosses such as Spicezee, a nectarine and plum hybrid. Decide on flavour and use first, then match the variety to your climate, because matching a nectarine to your winter chill is the single biggest factor in whether it crops well.

sections.heading: Matching the variety to your winter chill
sections.body: Nectarines are deciduous and need a spell of winter cold to wake up and crop properly. Chill is counted roughly as hours below about 7 degrees, and varieties differ widely: low-chill types such as Sunwright (around 150 hours) and White Satin (around 250 hours) suit warm and coastal districts, while most traditional nectarines want several hundred up to 800 or more hours, and old high-chill varieties such as Early Rivers want more again. Plant a high-chill variety where winters are too mild and budburst is delayed and drawn out, flowering and ripening turn uneven, and the fruit can come misshapen, so matching the variety's chill needs to your district is the most important choice you make.

sections.heading: Pollination: one tree is enough
sections.body: Nectarines are self-fertile, so a single tree will set a full crop with no second variety needed, which makes them an easy choice for a small garden. Bees and other insects still lift fruit set by moving pollen around the flowers, so a garden that is friendly to pollinators tends to crop a little more heavily. There is no common pollen-sterile nectarine to catch you out (unlike the odd peach, such as J.H. Hale), so almost any single named tree will fruit on its own.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Give nectarines full sun and deep, free-draining soil at a slightly acid pH of about 6.0 to 6.5. They resent wet feet, so never plant in a low spot where water pools, and lift the tree onto a low mound if your soil is heavy clay. Being deciduous, bare-rooted trees are sold and planted while dormant through late autumn and winter, which is the cheapest way to start (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Pruning and thinning
sections.body: Nectarines fruit on wood that grew the previous summer, so they need a firm prune every winter to keep making fresh fruiting wood, trained to an open vase shape that lets light and air into the tree. In spring, thin the young fruit hard, to about one nectarine every 15 centimetres (a hand-width) along the branch and finishing before the stones harden: it feels brutal, but the earlier you thin the larger the remaining fruit, while a crowded tree gives small, marked fruit and risks snapping limbs under the load. Thinning pays off even more on nectarines than peaches, because the bare, glossy skin shows every blemish, so a well-spaced, airy tree gives cleaner, larger, better-coloured fruit.

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Water deeply and regularly once growth starts, and treat three windows as make or break: the fortnight either side of flowering, the four weeks before harvest while the fruit makes its final swell, and December to March, when the tree builds the wood that carries next year's crop. A thirsty tree drops fruit and ripens it small, and there is no recovering the size later. Nectarines are hungry trees: the Queensland low-chill program feeds a mixed 12:5:14 N:P:K fertiliser at about 435 grams per mature open-vase tree in late winter and again in early autumn, with smaller top-ups (around 185 grams) in late spring and midsummer, and eases the nitrogen off late in the season so the wood hardens before winter. Mulch well to hold soil moisture, keep the soil slightly acid (about pH 5.5 to 6.5), and never let the roots sit wet.

sections.heading: Harvest and handling
sections.body: Nectarines ripen over summer, and the Australian season as a whole runs from about late September in the warm subtropical north to March in cool districts, depending on variety. Pick when the background colour shifts from green toward yellow or cream, the fruit gives slightly to a gentle squeeze, and it smells fragrant; do not go by the red blush, which colours up well before the fruit is ripe and only dulls as it matures. Nectarines gain little sugar once picked, so leave them on the tree until they are nearly ready, and handle them gently, because the smooth skin bruises far more easily than you expect.

sections.heading: Brown rot, leaf curl and the smooth skin
sections.body: The fuzz-free skin is what makes a nectarine, but it also offers the fruit a little less protection, so nectarines are more prone than peaches to a couple of troubles. Brown rot, a fungus that rots flowers and fruit in warm, wet spells near harvest, is the one nectarines are most susceptible to, so prune for airflow, thin the fruit, clear away mummified fruit, and protect the ripening crop with a registered fungicide in a wet season. Bacterial spot, which pits the skin and blotches the leaves, also hits nectarines harder than peaches, so in humid districts lean on more resistant varieties. Peach leaf curl, the fungus that puckers and reddens the new spring leaves, behaves just as it does on peaches: spray copper or lime sulfur at bud swell in late winter, before the buds open, because once the leaves are out it is too late for that season. Thrips and wind can scar or silver the bare skin too, which a sheltered, airy site keeps to a minimum.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted tree rather than a seedling: nectarines do not come true from seed, and a grafted tree fruits sooner and to type. Named varieties are worked onto peach rootstocks (Nemaguard in temperate districts, or warm-climate rootstocks such as Okinawa and Flordaguard in low-chill areas), with the rootstock helping the tree cope with local soil and nematodes. Bare-rooted trees in winter are the cheapest way in, genetic-dwarf forms suit pots and small gardens, and above all pick a variety whose chill needs match your climate. The in-stock table above shows the named varieties available right now and what they cost.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a peach and a nectarine?
faqs.a: A nectarine is a smooth-skinned peach, the very same species, with a fuzz-free skin caused by a single recessive gene. It is not a peach crossed with a plum, a common myth; it grows, flowers and crops exactly like a peach, with flesh that is usually a touch firmer and more aromatic. Anything true of growing peaches applies to nectarines too.

faqs.q: How long until a nectarine tree fruits?
faqs.a: Nectarines are quick croppers. A grafted tree often carries a little fruit in its second year and bears well by its third or fourth, and a bare-rooted tree planted in winter establishes fast and is the cheapest way to start.

faqs.q: Can I grow a nectarine tree in a pot?
faqs.a: Yes. Choose a genetic-dwarf variety such as Nectazee, dwarf Fantasia or Crimson Baby; they stay compact and crop in a large container in full sun with steady water and feeding. A pot also lets you stand the tree in the sunniest, most open spot and move it under cover during a wet spell near harvest, which helps keep brown rot off the fruit.

faqs.q: Do nectarines keep well once picked?
faqs.a: Not for long. A ripe nectarine is at its best within a few days and marks easily, so harvest into a single layer, refrigerate any you cannot eat soon, and bring it back to room temperature for the full flavour. Fruit picked a little firm will soften on the bench over a day or two, though it will not get much sweeter, which is why it pays to leave it hanging as long as you safely can.

WA.intro: Growing nectarines in Western Australia. The cooler hills and southern districts carry the winter chill nectarines want, the warm Swan coastal plain suits low-chill varieties, and WA's dry summers are kinder to a nectarine's brown-rot-prone skin than the humid east, while quarantine rules shape where you can buy a tree.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: South-west WA has warm, dry summers that ripen nectarines well, but winter chill varies a lot across the region. The coastal plain around Perth is mild, so it favours low and medium-chill varieties, while the Darling Range and the cooler Southern Forests carry enough chill for traditional types. The dry summer air is a real advantage here, because it keeps brown rot, the disease a smooth-skinned nectarine is most prone to, far lower than in the humid eastern states.

sections.heading: Where nectarines grow in WA
sections.body: The orchard district of Donnybrook in the south-west is WA's best-known stone-fruit and apple area, and the Perth Hills around Karragullen, Pickering Brook and Bickley grow stone fruit close to the city, on the Darling Range ground the Stoneville research station was set up to serve. The cooler Southern Forests around Manjimup and Pemberton carry more winter chill, while the warm Swan Valley ripens early.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: WA's warm summers bring nectarines on steadily. Low-chill varieties on the Swan coastal plain ripen from late spring, while the hills, Donnybrook and the Southern Forests run through summer and into autumn, giving the state a long season across its districts.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: Mediterranean fruit fly is endemic across WA and is a primary pest of soft stone fruit, and a smooth, fuzz-free nectarine is an even easier target for an egg-laying female, so most growers net trees or bait for it. Queensland fruit fly is a declared, prohibited pest in WA that is eradicated whenever it is detected, rather than tolerated, so Medfly is the fly to plan around here. The dry summers keep brown rot lower than in the east, but leaf curl still appears in wet winters, so keep up the late-winter copper spray.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA quarantine means live plants must be permitted on the Western Australian Organism List and are usually certified and treated before they can enter the state, and stone fruit (Prunus) nursery stock is tightly controlled for biosecurity. Many eastern nurseries therefore will not post nectarine trees to WA, so the in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship here.

faqs.q: Which nectarine varieties suit Perth and the coastal plain?
faqs.a: On the warm Swan coastal plain, choose low-chill varieties such as Sunwright, White Satin or Sun Snow. In the cooler Perth Hills and the Southern Forests you can also grow higher-chill types like Early Rivers and Goldmine, which crop poorly where winters are too mild.

faqs.q: Can nectarines cope with Perth's sandy soils?
faqs.a: Yes, as long as you fix the drainage and feeding. Dig in plenty of compost, mulch thickly, and use a soil wetting agent if the sand has turned water-repellent, then feed little and often, because nutrients leach quickly through sand. The sharp drainage actually suits a tree that hates wet feet.

QLD.intro: Growing nectarines in Queensland. The Granite Belt has the winter chill for traditional nectarines, the warm coast and subtropics need low-chill varieties bred for mild winters, and the humid summers make a nectarine's smooth skin harder to protect from brown rot than a fuzzy peach.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Coastal and subtropical south-east Queensland, and the tropics further north, are low in winter chill, so traditional high-chill nectarines fail there. Low-chill varieties, bred from the Florida program, made warm-climate stone fruit possible and are the right choice for most of the state. Queensland's humid summers also favour brown rot, which hits a fuzz-free nectarine harder than a peach, so disease management matters more here. The Granite Belt is the cool exception where traditional varieties also thrive.

sections.heading: Where nectarines grow in QLD
sections.body: The Granite Belt around Stanthorpe and Applethorpe, high on the New England Tableland at around 800 metres with real winter chill and frost, is Queensland's deciduous-fruit district. Low-chill nectarines are grown in the warmer south-east and Moreton districts, the Burnett, and on the Atherton Tableland in the far north.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Coastal low-chill nectarines are among the earliest in Australia: the warm subtropics start picking in spring and run into early summer. Up on the Granite Belt, traditional high-chill varieties ripen later, through summer.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is native throughout eastern Queensland and is the key pest, so netting, baiting and prompt clean-up of fallen fruit are essential. The humid summers favour brown rot of the fruit (worse on a smooth nectarine than a peach) and leaf curl in spring, so a fungicide program matters far more here than in dry inland districts.

faqs.q: Will nectarines fruit on the Queensland coast?
faqs.a: Yes, but only low-chill varieties such as Sunwright, Sun Snow or White Satin. The warm coast does not get the winter chill that traditional nectarines need, so high-chill types flower poorly and crop badly there.

faqs.q: Is a nectarine or a low-chill peach the safer bet in the humid subtropics?
faqs.a: Both need a low-chill variety, but in the wettest, most humid coastal spots a fuzzy peach shrugs off brown rot and skin marking a little better than a bare nectarine. If you have your heart set on nectarines, give them your driest, airiest, sunniest position and stay on top of fungicide near harvest.

NSW.intro: Growing nectarines in New South Wales. The state runs from the warm, low-chill coast to the cold, high-chill tablelands, so the right nectarine depends entirely on your district, and that spread of climates gives NSW one of the longest nectarine seasons in the country.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: NSW grows both traditional high-chill and low-chill stone fruit, and growers pick the variety to match the chill, soil and water of their area. The warm Sydney basin and coast suit low-chill types, while the cool Central Tablelands supply plenty of chill for traditional varieties. Match the variety to your district and nectarines are reliable across most of the state.

sections.heading: Where nectarines grow in NSW
sections.body: Bilpin, in the Hawkesbury hills near Sydney, is a famous fruit-growing district close to the city, and the cool Central Tablelands around Orange and Bathurst grow high-chill stone fruit. Batlow, in the Snowy Mountains foothills of the south, is better known for apples but carries the deep winter chill that the longest-season nectarines need. Choose the variety to suit the climb in altitude rather than the other way around.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: Warm coastal and low-chill orchards start picking in late spring, from about November, while the cooler tablelands such as Orange and Batlow ripen later, running into February and March. The state's spread of climates gives NSW one of the longest nectarine seasons in the country.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is native to the NSW coast and has spread through much of the inland, so it is the main pest of nectarines; net or bait the tree and pick up fallen fruit to break the breeding cycle. Leaf curl and brown rot turn up in wet springs, so keep to the late-winter leaf-curl spray and prune hard for airflow, which matters more for a smooth-skinned nectarine.

faqs.q: How do I protect nectarine blossom from frost in the NSW tablelands?
faqs.a: In the cold tablelands, early blossom can be caught by a spring frost. Plant on a slope or rise where cold air drains away, lean toward a later-flowering variety, and be ready to throw a cover over a small tree on a frosty night.

faqs.q: Which nectarine should I plant near Sydney rather than up on the tablelands?
faqs.a: Near the warm coast and the Sydney basin, stick to low-chill varieties such as Sunwright or Sun Snow that fruit on a mild winter. Up on the cold tablelands you can grow the high-chill heritage types like Early Rivers and Goldmine, which need the deeper cold to crop well.

VIC.intro: Growing nectarines in Victoria. Victoria's cold winters give nectarines all the chill they need and its warm, dry inland summers ripen excellent fruit, which is why the state grows about two thirds of Australia's nectarines.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Victoria's cool temperate winters easily supply the chill that traditional high-chill nectarines want, and its warm, dry summers, especially inland, ripen excellent fruit. The dry inland air suits a nectarine's brown-rot-prone smooth skin particularly well. The main watch-out is frost, which can damage early blossom and young fruit, so early-flowering varieties need a sheltered, frost-aware spot.

sections.heading: Where nectarines grow in VIC
sections.body: The Goulburn Valley around Shepparton and Cobram is Australia's stone-fruit heartland, while the hot, irrigated Sunraysia around Mildura and Swan Hill grows the earliest-season fruit. Together with Renmark just over the border, Swan Hill and the Goulburn Valley make up more than half of Australia's summer stonefruit. Tatura, in the Goulburn Valley, is the centre of Australian stone-fruit research, where the space-saving Tatura Trellis training system was developed.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Early districts like Sunraysia start in late spring, and the Goulburn Valley main crop runs through summer. Mid and late varieties carry Victoria's season well into autumn, so the state both opens and extends the southern nectarine supply.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is the key summer pest, and Victoria's irrigated fruit districts run strict area-wide fruit-fly baiting and produce-movement controls to protect their orchards. Brown rot causes serious losses in wet weather near harvest and is especially damaging to smooth-skinned nectarines, and frost protection for early blossom is part of the yearly routine.

faqs.q: Why is Victoria so good for nectarines?
faqs.a: Its cold winters easily supply the chill that traditional nectarines need, and its warm, dry summers ripen high-quality fruit while keeping disease pressure down. That combination is why Victoria grows the bulk of the national crop, centred on the Goulburn Valley and the irrigated north-west.

faqs.q: Will nectarines crop reliably around Melbourne?
faqs.a: Melbourne's milder, damper summers are less ideal than the dry inland, so expect a little more brown rot and somewhat lighter crops. A hot, open, north-facing spot, an airy annual prune and a variety suited to a milder winter give the best results in the city and the bayside suburbs.
